Walmart Black Friday — Customer Purchase Behavior Analysis¶

Business Case: Confidence Intervals & Central Limit Theorem¶

Author: Shivesh (Ethan) Sahu Date: June 2026

Project Overview¶

About Walmart¶

Walmart is an American multinational retail corporation operating a chain of supercenters, discount departmental stores, and grocery stores across the United States, with over 100 million customers worldwide.

Business Problem¶

The Management team at Walmart Inc. wants to analyze customer purchase behavior — specifically purchase amounts — against customer demographics to make better business decisions.

Central Question: Do women spend more on Black Friday than men? (Assuming a population of 50 million male and 50 million female customers)

Approach¶

Using the Central Limit Theorem (CLT) and Confidence Intervals, we will:

  • Determine whether spending habits differ between male and female customers
  • Extend the analysis to marital status (Married vs Unmarried) and age groups
  • Provide actionable, data-driven recommendations to Walmart leadership

Table of Contents¶

  1. Data Import & Initial Exploration
  2. Missing Values & Outlier Detection
  3. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
    • Univariate Analysis
    • Bivariate Analysis
    • Correlation Analysis
  4. Central Limit Theorem & Confidence Interval Analysis
    • 4.1 Gender: Male vs Female
    • 4.2 Marital Status: Married vs Unmarried
    • 4.3 Age Group Analysis
  5. Final Insights
  6. Business Recommendations

1. Importing Libraries and Loading the Dataset¶

We begin by importing the necessary libraries and loading the Walmart Black Friday dataset. We'll also do an initial inspection to understand the shape and structure of the data before any analysis.

from scipy import stats

1.4 Key Observations from Initial Exploration¶

Dataset Structure:

  • The dataset contains 550,068 transactions across 10 features — one row = one purchase event, not one customer. A single customer can appear multiple times.
  • Memory footprint: 42 MB — manageable in-memory with pandas.

Data Types:

  • 5 numeric columns: User_ID, Occupation, Marital_Status, Product_Category, Purchase
  • 5 categorical columns: Product_ID, Gender, Age, City_Category, Stay_In_Current_City_Years
  • Marital_Status is stored as int64 (0 = Single, 1 = Married) but is conceptually categorical.

Missing Values (preliminary):

  • All 10 columns show 550,068 non-null entries → no missing values detected at first glance. This will be confirmed formally in the next section.

Purchase Amount — most important column:

  • Mean: $9,264 vs Median: $8,047 → Mean > Median by ~$1,217
  • This tells us the distribution is right-skewed — a small number of very high-value purchases are pulling the average upward.
  • Range: $12 to $23,961 — wide spread, confirming the presence of outliers.
  • This right skew is exactly why we need the Central Limit Theorem later — it allows us to work with sample means which will be normally distributed, even though the raw purchase data is skewed.

Gender:

  • Male transactions: 414,259 (≈ 75%) vs Female: 135,809 (≈ 25%)
  • Important: this is a count of transactions, not customers. More male transactions does NOT mean women spend less — we must compare average purchase amounts, not totals.

Age & City:

  • Most active age group: 26–35 (219,587 transactions)
  • Most active city type: B (231,173 transactions)
  • Stay_In_Current_City_Years has 5 unique values (0, 1, 2, 3, 4+)
In [19]:
# Convert categorical columns from string to 'category' dtype
# This improves memory efficiency and makes groupby operations faster

cat_cols = ['Gender', 'Age', 'City_Category',
            'Stay_In_Current_City_Years', 'Marital_Status']

for col in cat_cols:
    df[col] = df[col].astype('category')

print("Updated Data Types:")
print(df.dtypes)
print(f"\nMemory after conversion: {df.memory_usage(deep=True).sum() / 1024**2:.1f} MB")

Updated Data Types:

Product_ID                         str
Gender                        category
Age                           category
Occupation                       int64
City_Category                 category
Stay_In_Current_City_Years    category
Marital_Status                category
Product_Category                 int64
Purchase                         int64
dtype: object

Memory after conversion: 49.8 MB

2. Missing Values and Outlier Detection¶

Missing Values¶

In [20]:
missing_count = df.isnull().sum()
missing_percentage = (df.isnull().sum()/len(df))*100

missing_df = pd.DataFrame({'Missing Values': missing_count, 'Missing Percentage': missing_percentage.round(2)})

print("\n Missing Value Report")
display(missing_df.sort_values(by='Missing Percentage', ascending=False))

if missing_count.sum() == 0:
    print("\nNo missing values")
else:
    print(f"\n Total Missing Values: {missing_count.sum()}")

Outlier Detection¶

In [24]:
# Method 1:
# Mean VS Median Comparison
mean_purchase = df['Purchase'].mean()
median_purchase = df['Purchase'].median()
diff = mean_purchase - median_purchase

print("\nMean VS Median Purchase")
print(f"Mean Purchase: {mean_purchase:.2f}")
print(f"Median Purchase: {median_purchase:.2f}")
print(f"Diff: {diff:.2f}")
print(f"\nMean is {(diff/mean_purchase*100):.1f}% higher than median, therefore Right-Skewed Distribution")

Mean VS Median Purchase
Mean Purchase: 9263.97
Median Purchase: 8047.00
Diff: 1216.97

Mean is 13.1% higher than median, therefore Right-Skewed Distribution
In [30]:
# Method 2:
# IQR Method to quantify outliers
Q1 = df['Purchase'].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = df['Purchase'].quantile(0.75)
IQR = Q3 - Q1

lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5*IQR
upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5*IQR

outliers = df[(df['Purchase'] > upper_bound) | (df['Purchase'] < lower_bound)]

print("=== IQR Outlier Analysis ===")
print(f"Q1 (25th percentile): ${Q1:,.2f}")
print(f"Q3 (75th percentile): ${Q3:,.2f}")
print(f"IQR:                  ${IQR:,.2f}")
print(f"\nLower Bound: ${lower_bound:,.2f}")
print(f"Upper Bound: ${upper_bound:,.2f}")
print(f"\nOutlier transactions: {len(outliers):,} ({len(outliers)/len(df)*100:.1f}% of data)")
print(f"Outlier purchase range: ${outliers['Purchase'].min():,} to ${outliers['Purchase'].max():,}")

=== IQR Outlier Analysis ===
Q1 (25th percentile): $5,823.00
Q3 (75th percentile): $12,054.00
IQR:                  $6,231.00

Lower Bound: $-3,523.50
Upper Bound: $21,400.50

Outlier transactions: 2,677 (0.5% of data)
Outlier purchase range: $21,401 to $23,961
In [33]:
# Method 3:
# BoxPlot -> Visual Conformation
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

# Boxplot
axes[0].boxplot(df['Purchase'], vert=False, patch_artist=True,
                boxprops=dict(facecolor='steelblue', alpha=0.7))
axes[0].set_title('Purchase Amount — Boxplot', fontsize=13)
axes[0].set_xlabel('Purchase Amount ($)')
axes[0].axvline(mean_purchase, color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'Mean: ${mean_purchase:,.0f}')
axes[0].axvline(median_purchase, color='green', linestyle='--', label=f'Median: ${median_purchase:,.0f}')
axes[0].legend()

# Distribution plot
axes[1].hist(df['Purchase'], bins=50, color='steelblue', alpha=0.7, edgecolor='white')
axes[1].axvline(mean_purchase, color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'Mean: ${mean_purchase:,.0f}')
axes[1].axvline(median_purchase, color='green', linestyle='--', label=f'Median: ${median_purchase:,.0f}')
axes[1].set_title('Purchase Amount — Distribution', fontsize=13)
axes[1].set_xlabel('Purchase Amount ($)')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Frequency')
axes[1].legend()

plt.suptitle('Outlier Detection: Purchase Column', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()

Observations — Missing Values & Outliers¶

Missing Values:

  • Zero missing values across all 550,068 rows and 10 columns — the dataset is complete.
  • No imputation or row-dropping required.

Outliers in Purchase Amount (IQR Method):

  • Q1 = 5,823 | Q3 = 12,054 | IQR = 6,231
  • Upper Bound = 21,400.50 (lower bound is negative — no lower outliers possible)
  • 2,677 transactions (0.5%) identified as outliers, ranging from 21,401 to 23,961
  • All outliers are on the upper end only — confirming a right-skewed distribution

Decision: Outliers are RETAINED. In retail transaction data, high-value purchases are legitimate business events — bulk buyers, premium products, gifting season. Removing them would distort the population-level CLT and Confidence Interval analysis that follows. The Central Limit Theorem will handle this skewness naturally by normalizing the distribution of sample means regardless of the original distribution shape.

Univariate Analysis - Categorical Variables¶

In [43]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(18, 11))

# Gender
sns.countplot(data=df, x='Gender', ax=axes[0,0],
              palette={'M':'steelblue', 'F':'coral'})
axes[0,0].set_title('Gender Distribution', fontsize=13)
axes[0,0].set_xlabel('Gender (M = Male, F = Female)')
for p in axes[0,0].patches:
    axes[0,0].annotate(f'{int(p.get_height()):,}',
                        (p.get_x() + p.get_width()/2, p.get_height()),
                        ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

# Age
age_order = ['0-17','18-25','26-35','36-45','46-50','51-55','55+']
sns.countplot(data=df, x='Age', ax=axes[0,1],
              palette='Blues_d', order=age_order)
axes[0,1].set_title('Age Group Distribution', fontsize=13)
axes[0,1].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)

# City Category
sns.countplot(data=df, x='City_Category', ax=axes[0,2], palette='Set2')
axes[0,2].set_title('City Category Distribution', fontsize=13)
axes[0,2].set_xlabel('City Category (A = Tier 1, B = Tier 2, C = Tier 3)')

# Stay in City Years
sns.countplot(data=df, x='Stay_In_Current_City_Years', ax=axes[1,0],
              palette='Blues_d')
axes[1,0].set_title('Years in Current City', fontsize=13)

# Marital Status
sns.countplot(data=df, x='Marital_Status', ax=axes[1,1],
              palette={'0':'steelblue', '1':'coral'})
axes[1,1].set_title('Marital Status Distribution', fontsize=13)
axes[1,1].set_xticklabels(['Single (0)', 'Married (1)'])

# Occupation
sns.countplot(data=df, x='Occupation', ax=axes[1,2], color='steelblue')
axes[1,2].set_title('Occupation Distribution', fontsize=13)
axes[1,2].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)

plt.suptitle('Univariate Analysis — Categorical Variables',
             fontsize=15, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(18, 12))

sns.boxplot(data=df, x='Gender', y='Purchase', ax=axes[0,0],
            palette={'M':'steelblue','F':'coral'})
axes[0,0].set_title('Purchase by Gender', fontsize=13)

# Age vs Purchase
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='Age', y='Purchase', ax=axes[0,1],
            palette='Blues_d', order=age_order)
axes[0,1].set_title('Purchase by Age Group', fontsize=13)
axes[0,1].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)

# City Category vs Purchase
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='City_Category', y='Purchase',
            ax=axes[0,2], palette='Set2')
axes[0,2].set_title('Purchase by City Category', fontsize=13)

# Marital Status vs Purchase
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='Marital_Status', y='Purchase',
            ax=axes[1,0], palette='Set2')
axes[1,0].set_title('Purchase by Marital Status', fontsize=13)
axes[1,0].set_xticklabels(['Single', 'Married'])

# Stay in City vs Purchase
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='Stay_In_Current_City_Years', y='Purchase',
            ax=axes[1,1], palette='Blues_d')
axes[1,1].set_title('Purchase by Years in City', fontsize=13)

# Gender + Age vs Purchase (mean)
gender_age = df.groupby(['Age', 'Gender'])['Purchase'].mean().reset_index()
for gender, color in [('M','steelblue'), ('F','coral')]:
    subset = gender_age[gender_age['Gender'] == gender]
    axes[1,2].plot(subset['Age'], subset['Purchase'],
                   marker='o', label=gender, color=color)
axes[1,2].set_title('Avg Purchase: Age × Gender', fontsize=13)
axes[1,2].set_xlabel('Age Group')
axes[1,2].set_ylabel('Average Purchase ($)')
axes[1,2].legend()
axes[1,2].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)

plt.suptitle('Bivariate Analysis — Demographics vs Purchase Amount',
             fontsize=15, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

g = sns.pairplot(sample_df[['Occupation', 'Product_Category', 'Purchase', 'Gender']],
                 hue='Gender',
                 palette={'M':'steelblue', 'F':'coral'},
                 plot_kws={'alpha': 0.3},
                 diag_kind='kde')
g.fig.suptitle('Pairplot — Numerical Features (Sample: 5,000 rows)',
               fontsize=13, y=1.02)
plt.show()

Business Insights from EDA¶

Non-Graphical Observations:

  • Gender: Dataset is male-dominated — 75% male (414,259) vs 25% female (135,809) transactions. This imbalance is in transaction count, not customer count.
  • Age: 26–35 is the most active group (219,587 transactions = 40% of data), followed by 36–45 and 18–25.
  • City: Tier B cities lead with 42% of transactions, suggesting Walmart's strongest urban middle market.
  • Marital Status: 59% Single (0), 41% Married (1).
  • Stay in City: 1 year is most common — many customers are relatively new residents.

Univariate Visual Observations:

  • Purchase distribution is right-skewed with a long tail — consistent with our earlier mean ($9,264) > median ($8,047) finding.
  • Product Category 1, 5, and 8 dominate transaction counts.

Bivariate Visual Observations:

  • Gender vs Purchase: Male and female purchase distributions are visually similar in spread — the actual mean difference needs CLT analysis to confirm significance.
  • Age vs Purchase: The 51–55 age group shows the highest median purchase. The 0–17 group has the lowest — expected given limited income.
  • City Category: City C customers show wider purchase spread than A or B.
  • Marital Status: Single and Married customers show nearly identical purchase distributions.

Correlation Observations:

  • Notable finding: Product_Category and Purchase show a -0.344 correlation — the strongest relationship in the dataset. Lower-numbered categories (1, 5, 8) tend to have higher purchase amounts.
  • All other numerical variables are near-zero correlated with Purchase.
  • This confirms group-level analysis (CLT + CI) is the right approach.

4. Central Limit Theorem & Confidence Interval Analysis¶

Core Concept: The CLT states that regardless of the shape of the original distribution, the distribution of sample means approaches a normal distribution as sample size (n) increases. This allows us to build Confidence Intervals (CIs), a range within which the true population mean is very likely to lie.

Formula used throughout this section:

  • Standard Error (SE) = Sample_Std / sqrt(Population_Size)
  • CI = Sample_Mean ± (z × SE)
  • z = 1.645 (90%) | 1.96 (95%) | 2.576 (99%)
  • Population size assumed = 50,000,000 (per the business problem)

Question 1: Are Women Spending More Per Transaction Than Men?¶

In [50]:
# Split data by gender
female_df = df[df['Gender'] == 'F']
male_df   = df[df['Gender'] == 'M']

female_mean = female_df['Purchase'].mean()
male_mean   = male_df['Purchase'].mean()
female_std  = female_df['Purchase'].std(ddof=1)
male_std    = male_df['Purchase'].std(ddof=1)

comparison = pd.DataFrame({
    'Female': [
        f"{len(female_df):,}",
        f"{female_mean:.2f}",
        f"{female_df['Purchase'].median():.2f}",
        f"{female_std:.2f}",
        f"{female_df['Purchase'].min():,}",
        f"{female_df['Purchase'].max():,}"
    ],
    'Male': [
        f"{len(male_df):,}",
        f"{male_mean:.2f}",
        f"{male_df['Purchase'].median():.2f}",
        f"{male_std:.2f}",
        f"{male_df['Purchase'].min():,}",
        f"{male_df['Purchase'].max():,}"
    ]
}, index=['Sample Size','Mean Purchase','Median Purchase',
          'Std Deviation','Min','Max'])

print("Gender-wise Purchase Statistics \n")
display(comparison)

diff = male_mean - female_mean
print(f"\nDifference (Male - Female): {diff:.2f}")
print(f"Men spend {(diff/female_mean*100):.1f}% more per transaction than women on average.")
print(f"\nPreliminary answer: NO — men spend MORE than women on average.")
print(f"But is this difference real for all 50M customers? -> CLT analysis will confirm.")

Gender-wise Purchase Statistics
Female Male
Sample Size 135,809 414,259
Mean Purchase 8734.57 9437.53
Median Purchase 7914.00 8098.00
Std Deviation 4767.23 5092.19
Min 12 12
Max 23,959 23,961 
Difference (Male - Female): 702.96
Men spend 8.0% more per transaction than women on average.

Preliminary answer: NO — men spend MORE than women on average.
But is this difference real for all 50M customers? -> CLT analysis will confirm.

Central Limit Theorem -> Demonstration¶

As sample size (n) increases, the distribution of sample means:

  1. Becomes more bell-shaped (normal) -> regardless of the original skewed distribution
  2. Becomes narrower -> the mean is estimated more precisely
  3. Stays centered at the true population mean

This is exactly why we can use sample data to make conclusions about 50 million customers.

In [51]:
np.random.seed(42)
sample_sizes = [30, 100, 500, 1000, 5000]
n_iterations = 1000

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 5, figsize=(22, 8))

for idx, n in enumerate(sample_sizes):
    f_means = [female_df['Purchase'].sample(n).mean() for _ in range(n_iterations)]
    m_means = [male_df['Purchase'].sample(n).mean() for _ in range(n_iterations)]

    # Female row
    axes[0, idx].hist(f_means, bins=30, color='coral', alpha=0.8, edgecolor='white')
    axes[0, idx].axvline(np.mean(f_means), color='darkred', linestyle='--', lw=2,
                          label=f'Mean: {np.mean(f_means):,.0f}')
    axes[0, idx].set_title(f'Female  n={n:,}', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')
    axes[0, idx].set_xlabel('Sample Mean')
    axes[0, idx].legend(fontsize=8)
    if idx == 0: axes[0, idx].set_ylabel('Frequency')

    # Male row
    axes[1, idx].hist(m_means, bins=30, color='steelblue', alpha=0.8, edgecolor='white')
    axes[1, idx].axvline(np.mean(m_means), color='darkblue', linestyle='--', lw=2,
                          label=f'Mean: {np.mean(m_means):,.0f}')
    axes[1, idx].set_title(f'Male  n={n:,}', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')
    axes[1, idx].set_xlabel('Sample Mean')
    axes[1, idx].legend(fontsize=8)
    if idx == 0: axes[1, idx].set_ylabel('Frequency')

plt.suptitle('Central Limit Theorem — Distribution of Sample Means\n'
             '(1,000 samples per size | Row 1: Female | Row 2: Male)',
             fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Confidence Intervals — Male vs Female¶

In [66]:
# Population size given in the problem
n_population = 50_000_000

# CI formula: mean +/- z * (std / sqrt(n))
def compute_ci(data, n_pop, confidence):
    sample_mean = data.mean()
    sample_std = data.std(ddof=1)
    std_error = sample_std / np.sqrt(n_pop)

    # z-scores for each confidence level
    z_values = {0.90: 1.645, 0.95: 1.96, 0.99: 2.576}
    z = z_values[confidence]

    margin = z * std_error
    lower = sample_mean - margin
    upper = sample_mean + margin

    return {'Mean': round(sample_mean, 4), 'Std': round(sample_std, 4),
            'SE': round(std_error, 6), 'Z': z, 'Margin': round(margin, 4),
            'Lower': round(lower, 4), 'Upper': round(upper, 4)}

# Female customers
print("Female Customers - Population 50 Million")
for conf in [0.90, 0.95, 0.99]:
    r = compute_ci(female_df['Purchase'], n_population, conf)
    print(f"{int(conf*100)}% CI : ({r['Lower']},  {r['Upper']})  Mean: {r['Mean']}")

print()

# Male customers
print("Male Customers - Population 50 Million")
for conf in [0.90, 0.95, 0.99]:
    r = compute_ci(male_df['Purchase'], n_population, conf)
    print(f"{int(conf*100)}% CI : ({r['Lower']},  {r['Upper']})  Mean: {r['Mean']}")

Female Customers - Population 50 Million
90% CI : (8733.4567,  8735.6748)  Mean: 8734.5658
95% CI : (8733.2444,  8735.8872)  Mean: 8734.5658
99% CI : (8732.8291,  8736.3025)  Mean: 8734.5658

Male Customers - Population 50 Million
90% CI : (9436.3414,  9438.7107)  Mean: 9437.526
95% CI : (9436.1146,  9438.9375)  Mean: 9437.526
99% CI : (9435.6709,  9439.3811)  Mean: 9437.526
In [54]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 6))

for idx, conf in enumerate([0.90, 0.95, 0.99]):
    f_ci = compute_ci(female_df['Purchase'], n_population, conf)
    m_ci = compute_ci(male_df['Purchase'], n_population, conf)

    means   = [f_ci['Mean'],   m_ci['Mean']]
    margins = [f_ci['Margin'], m_ci['Margin']]

    bars = axes[idx].bar(['Female', 'Male'], means,
                          color=['coral','steelblue'], alpha=0.8, width=0.5,
                          yerr=margins, capsize=10,
                          error_kw={'elinewidth':2, 'capthick':2, 'ecolor':'black'})

    for bar, ci in zip(bars, [f_ci, m_ci]):
        axes[idx].text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, ci['Mean'] + 200,
                       f"Mean: {ci['Mean']:.2f}\n[{ci['Lower']:.2f},\n {ci['Upper']:.2f}]",
                       ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=8.5)

    axes[idx].set_title(f'{int(conf*100)}% Confidence Interval', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
    axes[idx].set_ylabel('Average Purchase Amount')
    axes[idx].set_ylim(7000, max(means) + 1800)

    overlap = f_ci['Upper'] >= m_ci['Lower']
    status  = "OVERLAPPING" if overlap else "NON-OVERLAPPING"
    col     = 'red' if overlap else 'green'
    axes[idx].text(0.5, 0.04, status, transform=axes[idx].transAxes,
                   ha='center', fontsize=12, color=col, fontweight='bold',
                   bbox=dict(boxstyle='round', facecolor='lightyellow', alpha=0.9))

plt.suptitle('Confidence Intervals — Female vs Male Average Purchase\n'
             '(Population: 50 Million each | Error bars show CI width)',
             fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Are the CIs Overlapping? Business Implications¶

Finding:

  • The confidence intervals for male and female average purchase amounts are NON-OVERLAPPING at all confidence levels (90%, 95%, 99%).
  • The gap between the upper bound of female CI and lower bound of male CI is approximately 700 units — while each CI is only ±1-2 units wide.
  • This is a statistically significant result. We can say with 99% confidence that the true average male purchase is higher than the true average female purchase.

Why are the CIs so narrow? Because we are projecting to a population of 50 million customers. SE = Std / sqrt(50,000,000) — the denominator is huge, making SE tiny. This means even a small difference in means is highly significant at population scale.

Business Implications for Walmart:

  1. Men consistently and significantly spend more per Black Friday transaction. This is not a sampling fluke — it holds for the entire population.
  2. Walmart should design gender-targeted promotions to lift female spending. Even closing 25% of the gap (from 8,734 to 8,909) across 50M female customers adds billions in revenue.
  3. High-value product placement and bundling strategies should differ by gender.
  4. Female customers may prefer different product categories — Walmart should analyse which categories drive female purchases and promote them aggressively.

Married vs Unmarried — Confidence Interval Analysis¶

In [57]:
single_df  = df[df['Marital_Status'].astype(str) == '0']
married_df = df[df['Marital_Status'].astype(str) == '1']

print(" Marital Status :- Purchase Statistics \n")
for name, grp in [('Single (0)', single_df), ('Married (1)', married_df)]:
    print(f"{name}: n={len(grp):,} | Mean={grp['Purchase'].mean():.2f} | "
          f"Std={grp['Purchase'].std():.2f}")

print("\n Confidence Intervals ")
for name, grp in [('Single', single_df), ('Married', married_df)]:
    print(f"\n{name}:")
    for conf in [0.90, 0.95, 0.99]:
        r = compute_ci(grp['Purchase'], n_population, conf)
        print(f"  {int(conf*100)}% CI: [{r['Lower']:.4f}, {r['Upper']:.4f}]  "
              f"Mean: {r['Mean']:.4f}")

# Overlap check
s_ci = compute_ci(single_df['Purchase'],  n_population, 0.95)
m_ci = compute_ci(married_df['Purchase'], n_population, 0.95)
overlap = s_ci['Upper'] >= m_ci['Lower'] or m_ci['Upper'] >= s_ci['Lower']
print(f"\n95% CI Overlap: {'YES — NOT significant' if overlap else 'NO — Statistically significant'}")
print(f"Single mean:  {s_ci['Mean']:.2f} | Married mean: {m_ci['Mean']:.2f}")
print(f"Difference:   {abs(s_ci['Mean'] - m_ci['Mean']):.2f}")

Age Group Analysis — Confidence Interval Analysis¶

In [62]:
age_order = ['0-17','18-25','26-35','36-45','46-50','51-55','55+']

print(" Age Group :- Purchase Statistics & 95% CIs \n")
age_results = {}
for age in age_order:
    grp = df[df['Age'].astype(str) == age]['Purchase']
    ci  = compute_ci(grp, n_population, 0.95)
    age_results[age] = ci
    print(f"{age:<8}: n={len(grp):>6,} | Mean={ci['Mean']:>8.2f} | "
          f"95% CI: [{ci['Lower']:.2f}, {ci['Upper']:.2f}]")

In [63]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 7))

colors_age = ['#3498db','#2ecc71','#e67e22','#e74c3c','#9b59b6','#1abc9c','#f39c12']

for i, (age, color) in enumerate(zip(age_order, colors_age)):
    ci = age_results[age]
    ax.errorbar(ci['Mean'], i,
                xerr=[[ci['Mean'] - ci['Lower']], [ci['Upper'] - ci['Mean']]],
                fmt='o', color=color, capsize=8, capthick=3,
                elinewidth=3, markersize=12, label=age)
    ax.text(ci['Mean'] + 8, i + 0.15,
            f"Mean: {ci['Mean']:.1f}  [{ci['Lower']:.2f}, {ci['Upper']:.2f}]",
            va='bottom', fontsize=9)

ax.set_yticks(range(len(age_order)))
ax.set_yticklabels(age_order, fontsize=11)
ax.set_xlabel('Average Purchase Amount', fontsize=12)
ax.set_title('95% Confidence Intervals by Age Group\n'
             '(Population: 50 Million | Points = Mean | Lines = CI)',
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
ax.grid(axis='x', alpha=0.3)
ax.set_xlim(min(r['Lower'] for r in age_results.values()) - 100,
            max(r['Upper'] for r in age_results.values()) + 400)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observations :- Age Group Analysis¶

  • Age groups have non-overlapping CIs, meaning spending differences across age groups are statistically significant at the population level.
  • The 51-55 age group has the highest average purchase :- likely due to higher disposable income in peak earning years.
  • The 0-17 group has the lowest average purchase :- expected given limited financial independence.
  • The 26-35 group has the highest transaction count (40% of all purchases) but is not the highest spender per transaction :- volume does not equal value.

Business Implication: Walmart should tailor Black Friday promotions differently by life stage:

  • Younger shoppers (18-25): discount-driven, focus on price-sensitive products
  • Prime earners (36-55): premium products, convenience, quality over price
  • Seniors (55+): fewer transactions but moderate spend :- loyalty programs

5. Final Insights¶

Distribution and Variable Relationships¶

Purchase Amount Distribution:

  • The raw purchase distribution is right-skewed (mean 9,264 > median 8,047). However, once we apply the CLT and look at the distribution of sample means, it becomes perfectly normal, this is the CLT in action.
  • The skewness in raw data does NOT affect our population-level conclusions because we are working with sample means, not individual values.

Gender:

  • Male customers generate 75% of all transactions but also spend significantly more per transaction (mean 9,437 vs 8,734 for females).
  • This is NOT just a volume effect, the per-transaction spend is genuinely higher for men, confirmed by non-overlapping CIs at 99% confidence.
  • Possible reason: men may purchase higher-priced electronics and gadgets, while women may buy more lower-priced household items.

Marital Status:

  • Contrary to what one might expect, marital status has NO statistically significant effect on purchase amount. Single (9,265) vs Married (9,261) differ by just 4.73.
  • The overlapping CIs confirm this is noise, not signal.
  • Implication: Walmart should NOT segment its Black Friday strategy by marital status, the spending behavior is identical between both groups.

Age Groups:

  • All age group CIs are non-overlapping, spending differs significantly across life stages.
  • 51-55 spends the most (9,534), likely due to peak earning years and fewer financial obligations compared to 36-45 age group.
  • 0-17 spends the least (8,933), expected given limited financial independence.
  • Interestingly, 26-35 has the highest transaction COUNT but is not the highest spender per transaction. This age group drives volume, not value.

City Category:

  • City B dominates transaction count (42%), Walmart's Tier 2 urban market is its largest customer base.
  • City C shows the widest purchase spread, suggesting more diverse buying behavior in Tier 3 cities.

Product Category:

  • The -0.344 correlation between Product_Category and Purchase indicates that lower-numbered categories (1, 5, 8) are higher-value product lines.
  • These categories should receive premium placement during Black Friday campaigns.

Generalizing to the Population of 50 Million Customers¶

Using the CLT, we have established the following with 99% confidence:

Group Population Mean CI (99%) Significant?
Female (50M) [8732.83, 8736.30] —
Male (50M) [9435.67, 9439.38] YES — higher than female
Single (50M) [9264.08, 9267.74] —
Married (50M) [9259.35, 9263.00] NO — overlaps with single
Age 51-55 (50M) [9533.40, 9536.22] YES — highest age group
Age 0-17 (50M) [8932.05, 8934.88] YES — lowest age group

Key takeaway: The most reliable predictor of higher Black Friday spending is being male and being in the 46-55 age bracket. Marital status is irrelevant.

6. Business Recommendations¶

Based on the CLT and Confidence Interval analysis of 550,068 Black Friday transactions,here are actionable recommendations for Walmart leadership:

Recommendation 1 :- Close the Gender Spending Gap¶

Men spend 703 more per transaction than women on average, confirmed for the entire population. Walmart should create female-focused Black Friday deals on categories women buy most (household, apparel, beauty). Even a 10% increase in female spend per transaction across 50M customers would generate billions in additional revenue.

Recommendation 2 :- Invest Heavily in the 46-55 Age Segment¶

The 51-55 and 46-50 age groups are the highest spenders per transaction. Walmart should offer premium product bundles, early access deals, and loyalty rewards targeted at this segment. These customers have the budget and willingness to spend.

Recommendation 3 :- Drive Volume from the 26-35 Segment¶

The 26-35 group makes the most transactions (40% of all purchases) but does not spend the most per transaction. Upselling and bundling strategies during checkout, such as "frequently bought together" offers, can increase their basket size.

Recommendation 4 :- Do Not Segment by Marital Status¶

Analysis confirms that single and married customers spend virtually the same amount. Any marketing budget allocated to marital-status-based campaigns would be wasted. Redirect that budget to gender and age-based targeting instead.

Recommendation 5 :- Focus Black Friday Campaigns on Tier B Cities¶

City B generates 42% of all transactions. Walmart should concentrate its Black Friday promotional spend, inventory stocking, and staffing in Tier B cities to maximise return on investment.

Recommendation 6 :- Promote High-Value Product Categories Prominently¶

Product Categories 1, 5, and 8 drive the highest transaction counts and correlate with higher purchase amounts. These should receive the best floor placement, homepage banner ads online, and the deepest promotional discounts to drive maximum revenue during Black Friday.
